tagged_story_for_tts: <[silence:3400ms]>Superheated gas. <[silence:1300ms]> Charged particles dancing.<[silence:1300ms]>"Plasma physics" studies matter so hot its atoms split—electrons flee, leaving behind naked nuclei, all swirling in an electric soup.<[silence:700ms]>It’s like a campfire, but instead of wood burning, the flames themselves become a storm of tiny magnets and sparks.<[silence:1300ms]>Lightning is plasma—a jagged river of electrons crashing through air, heating it to 30,000°C in microseconds.<[silence:700ms]>Stars? Giant plasma balls.<[silence:700ms]>Fusion reactors mimic them, squeezing hydrogen plasma until nuclei fuse, releasing energy like the Sun does.<[silence:1300ms]>The trick?<[silence:700ms]>Plasma hates being contained.<[silence:700ms]>Tokamaks use magnetic fields as invisible walls, shaping plasma into donuts spinning at millions of kilometers per hour.<[silence:700ms]>One misstep—turbulence, a weak spot—and the plasma touches the metal vessel, cooling instantly, like ice on lava.<[silence:1300ms]>Auroras happen when solar plasma—ejected from the Sun—slams into Earth’s magnetic field.<[silence:700ms]>The collision excites oxygen and nitrogen, painting the sky green or purple.<[silence:2000ms]>Three related subjects are energy sources, magnetic fields and solar winds.<[silence:1200ms]>
clean_story: Superheated gas.  Charged particles dancing. "Plasma physics" studies matter so hot its atoms split—electrons flee, leaving behind naked nuclei, all swirling in an electric soup. It’s like a campfire, but instead of wood burning, the flames themselves become a storm of tiny magnets and sparks. Lightning is plasma—a jagged river of electrons crashing through air, heating it to 30,000°C in microseconds. Stars? Giant plasma balls. Fusion reactors mimic them, squeezing hydrogen plasma until nuclei fuse, releasing energy like the Sun does. The trick?Plasma hates being contained. Tokamaks use magnetic fields as invisible walls, shaping plasma into donuts spinning at millions of kilometers per hour. One misstep—turbulence, a weak spot—and the plasma touches the metal vessel, cooling instantly, like ice on lava. Auroras happen when solar plasma—ejected from the Sun—slams into Earth’s magnetic field. The collision excites oxygen and nitrogen, painting the sky green or purple. Three related subjects are energy sources, magnetic fields and solar winds.
